params.text:
Why Anonymization Matters

Even though you have patient consent, protecting identity is crucial:

  • Prevents harm - Stigma can affect employment, relationships
  • Legal requirement - Many jurisdictions mandate de-identification
  • Ethical obligation - Minimizes data even with consent
  • Trust preservation - Patients must trust the system

Rule: No identifying information should be visible in any submitted image.

ALWAYS Remove:

  • Faces and facial features
  • Names on wristbands
  • Tattoos (unless the tattoo IS the lesion)
  • Jewelry with initials
  • Clothing with logos/text
  • Background items that identify location
  • Unique birthmarks
  • Scars that could identify patient

Techniques:

  • Cropping: Frame tightly around lesion
  • Black bars: Cover identifying areas
  • Blurring: Use photo editing tools
  • Neutral backgrounds: Plain sheets or drapes
  • Check twice: Review before submission
  • When in doubt: Don't submit - retake

Common Mistakes:

Problem 1: Photo shows patient's hand with wedding ring visible
→ Solution: Remove jewelry or crop to show only lesion area

Problem 2: Hospital ID band visible in corner of frame
→ Solution: Remove wristband before photography or crop out

Problem 3: Unique constellation of moles visible near lesion
→ Solution: These could identify patient; crop or blur the area

Problem 4: Window in background shows recognizable landmark
→ Solution: Use neutral backdrop, close curtains, or change position

Remember: If you can identify the patient from the photo, someone else might too.

Data Collection System Protections:

  • Secure transmission: Encrypted upload to authorized servers
  • Access controls: Only authorized personnel can view raw images
  • Audit trails: Who accessed what and when
  • De-identification: Automatic removal of metadata that could identify
  • Storage security: Secure, backed-up, access-controlled storage

Your Responsibilities:

  • Ensure photos on your device are deleted after secure upload
  • Don't store images on personal devices long-term
  • Don't share images via email or messaging apps
  • Report any data breaches immediately
  • Follow your institution's data protection policies

Knowledge Check

Which of the following does NOT need to be removed from a clinical photo?

params.question: Which of the following does NOT need to be removed from a clinical photo before submission?

answers.text: Patient's wristband with name and ID
answers.tip: Incorrect. Wristbands contain identifying information and must be removed or covered.

answers.text: A mole that is part of the lesion being documented
answers.tip: Correct! Clinical features that are part of the condition being documented should remain visible.

answers.text: Tattoo on patient's arm near the lesion
answers.tip: Incorrect. Tattoos can be identifying and should be covered unless the tattoo itself is the clinical finding.
Incorrect. Background elements that identify location should be avoided or removed.
